pattern: A circle of alternating tiles is only ever broken at the handful of seams where two neighbors share a color, so the circle splits into a small number of maximal alternating runs -- counting windows of a given size becomes arithmetic over those run lengths instead of scanning every tile, and an edit only ever touches the one or two seams next to it.
hints: Say there's a 'seam' at position i whenever tile i shares its color with the tile before it (wrapping around at the start). Seams are the only places an alternating run can end.

If there are zero seams the whole circle alternates perfectly, so every one of the n starting positions gives a valid window of any requested size up to n. Otherwise, cutting the circle at each seam gives you a handful of linear runs whose lengths sum to n.

For a run of length L, the number of windows of size s that fit entirely inside it is max(0, L - s + 1) -- sum that over every run for a size query. An update only ever flips the seam status at the edited tile and the tile right after it, so you don't need to rebuild anything else.

edge_inputs.description: an update followed by a size query
a size query, then an update that doesn't change anything, then another size query
minimum-length circle, a single size query
perfectly alternating circle with zero seams -- every rotation counts
all one color -- no alternating window of any size exists
query size equal to the full circle length
several updates in a row before any size query is asked
an update at index 0 must correctly affect the wraparound seam with the last tile

stress.description: random colors with a random mix of size queries and updates, kept to a moderate circle length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(4, min(size, 200))
    colors = [rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)]
    q_count = max(1, min(size, 200))
    queries = []
    for _ in range(q_count):
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            s = rng.randint(3, n)
            queries.append([1, s])
        else:
            idx = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
            c = rng.randint(0, 1)
            queries.append([2, idx, c])
    return [colors, queries]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def numberOfAlternatingGroups(self, colors, queries):
        n = len(colors)
        colors = list(colors)
        brk = [colors[i] == colors[i - 1] for i in range(n)]
        answer = []
        for q in queries:
            if q[0] == 2:
                idx, color = q[1], q[2]
                colors[idx] = color
                brk[idx] = (colors[idx] == colors[idx - 1])
                nxt = (idx + 1) % n
                brk[nxt] = (colors[nxt] == colors[(nxt - 1) % n])
            else:
                s = q[1]
                positions = [i for i in range(n) if brk[i]]
                if not positions:
                    answer.append(n)
                    continue
                total = 0
                k = len(positions)
                for j in range(k):
                    start = positions[j]
                    end = positions[(j + 1) % k]
                    length = end - start if end > start else end - start + n
                    if length >= s:
                        total += length - s + 1
                answer.append(total)
        return answer

complexity.time: O(n) per query in this implementation (a Fenwick-tree-over-run-lengths refinement gets updates and queries to O(log n))
